Summary: ESR1-centered. In ER+ breast cancer, ESR1 (encoding ERα) is required for estrogen-driven proliferation and is a focal target of endocrine therapy; in metastatic ER+ tumors acquired somatic alterations in ESR1 commonly arise after long-term aromatase inhibitor or tamoxifen treatment. Point mutations in the ligand-binding domain (notably at Y537 and D538) stabilize helix 12 in an active conformation, which enables coactivator binding in the absence of ligand and decreases affinity for estrogen and tamoxifen, thereby producing ligand-independent ER transcriptional activity and causing resistance to aromatase inhibitors with reduced sensitivity to tamoxifen and fulvestrant. Separately, ESR1 in-frame fusions that retain the N-terminal DNA-binding domain but lack the LBD drive estrogen-independent ER transcription, proliferation, motility, and metastasis; these fusion-driven tumors remained sensitive to CDK4/6 inhibition. ESR1 amplifications are less frequent and their association with resistance is unclear.
References: Hanker, A. B., Sudhan, D. R., & Arteaga, C. L. (2020). Overcoming Endocrine Resistance in Breast Cancer. Cancer Cell, 37(4), 496-513. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ccell.2020.03.009
